summary: This is a fictional narrative chapter following a protagonist who finds a detective's business card at his door and spirals into paranoia about why Nassau County police would visit his Brooklyn home. Despite not having committed any crime, he obsesses over police interrogation tactics and surveillance possibilities. He eventually calms himself, attributing the visit to his father's business dealings, and goes to a friend's basement party where they smoke weed and watch videos. The chapter explores the psychological weight of unexpected police contact and the comfort found in mundane social rituals.
key_points: The narrator applies true crime knowledge about not talking to police while recognizing his own vulnerability to manipulation
Paranoia feeds on itself: not knowing why police visited is worse than knowing you committed a crime
The chapter contrasts surveillance anxiety with the grounding normalcy of friendship and teenage rituals
A stoner's 'what if we're the mouse' observation gets dismissed, but the narrator's paranoia suggests he feels exactly like that mouse
Physical security rituals (three locks) provide psychological comfort against abstract threats
money_quotes: I had watched enough true crime to know that you should never talk to the police. And I wasn't arrogant enough to believe that I was different.
Another system carefully crafted based on years and years of 'user feedback' designed to manipulate you into doing what it wants.
If I did commit a crime this would all be a lot easier, I'd know why they were here and what they wanted and could plan my next move accordingly.
From both a fear of monitored communication and a fear of spreading my paranoia, this wasn't worth texting her about.
Even though I couldn't see it, it reminded me that there was a society out there.
With the blanket of those three locks, a bunch of beers, and a couple hits of the Volcano, drifted off to sleep.
key_insight: The absence of guilt doesn't protect you from the psychological weight of institutional power - uncertainty about why you're being watched can be more destabilizing than knowing you've done something wrong.
